PARAGRAPH 1
Here is a python function designed to help you analyze a list of historical dates and return only those that fall within a specified century. As a researcher, you often deal with a large dataset of dates in the format "YYYY-MM-DD", and you need an efficient way to extract dates corresponding to a certain century. The function takes two arguments: a list of date strings and an integer specifying the century. The function returns a list of dates that belong to the given century. Input involves a list of strings representing dates in the format "YYYY-MM-DD" and an integer representing the century. For example, 19 for the 19th century which includes the years 1801-1900. Output is a list of strings representing dates that fall within the specified century. Consider this example where dates are "1789-07-14", "1801-01-01", "1899-12-31", "1900-01-01", "2000-12-31" and century is 19. The expected output is "1801-01-01", "1899-12-31". Please ensure that your solution is efficient and can handle a large list of dates. The logic relies on calculating the start and end year for any given century. A century begins at year (century minus one) times one hundred plus one. It ends at year times one hundred. For the 19th century, the start year is one thousand eight hundred one. The end year is one thousand nine hundred. We parse each date string to extract the year component. Then we check if that year falls within the calculated range. If it does, we add the original date string to our result list. This approach avoids converting strings to date objects which can be slower for very large datasets. String slicing is very fast in python. We take the first four characters of each date string to get the year. We convert that substring to an integer for comparison. This method is robust and handles the specific format "YYYY-MM-DD" perfectly. It ensures that dates like "1900-01-01" are correctly excluded from the 19th century list because 1900 is not in the range 1801 to 1900. Wait, 1900
